name: Citizenship

sourceRefs.label: 2026_q page 35 Q34 (ways of losing citizenship — all 3 statements true, with answer key)
2026_q page 116 Q137 (correctly-paired mode of acquiring citizenship — only Descent pairing correct, with answer key)

researchNotes: 2 real past-paper hits, both unusually high-value: a 'which statements are true' (loss of citizenship) and a 'correctly paired' trap (modes of acquisition) — both confirm TNPSC tests citizenship via STATEMENT-COMBINATION and MIS-PAIRING traps rather than plain definitions. The Q137 trap is the most instructive: it deliberately swaps the standard textbook descriptions of Registration, Naturalisation, and Incorporation-of-Territory across each other, leaving only the Descent pairing correct — a reader who recognizes only 'sounds roughly right' phrasing for all three would get this wrong; the fix is to know each mode's description precisely enough to catch a swapped one.

reading.intro: Covers Part II of the Constitution (Articles 5-11) and the Citizenship Act, 1955. Real exam evidence shows TNPSC favors testing this via true/false statement combinations and deliberately mis-paired mode-definitions over plain recall.

qa.question: Which Articles of the Constitution deal with citizenship, and what do they broadly cover?
qa.answer: Articles 5-11 (Part II). Article 5: citizenship at the Constitution's commencement (domicile in India). Article 6: rights of those who migrated from Pakistan before 19 July 1948. Article 7: rights of those who migrated to Pakistan and later returned. Article 8: citizenship rights of persons of Indian origin residing outside India. Article 9: voluntarily acquiring another country's citizenship ends Indian citizenship. Article 10: continuance of citizenship rights subject to law. Article 11: empowers PARLIAMENT to make laws regulating citizenship — exercised via the Citizenship Act, 1955.

qa.question: What are the FIVE modes of acquiring Indian citizenship under the Citizenship Act, 1955?
qa.answer: Birth, Descent, Registration, Naturalisation, and Incorporation of Territory.

qa.question: Real exam: What is Citizenship by Descent, and what date range governs the most commonly tested version of this rule?
qa.answer: A person born OUTSIDE India is a citizen by descent if a parent was a citizen of India at the time of birth. The classic tested date range: born outside India on or after 26 January 1950 but before 10 December 1992 — citizen by descent if the FATHER was an Indian citizen at the time of birth (rules for births after 1992 require either parent, with added conditions).

qa.question: Real exam trap: What is Citizenship by Registration actually defined as (distinct from Naturalisation and Incorporation of Territory)?
qa.answer: Registration applies to specific categories (e.g., a person of Indian origin, or a person married to an Indian citizen, ordinarily resident in India for a qualifying period, or minor children of Indian citizens) applying for registration — NOT simply 'any foreigner applying.' A real exam question deliberately swapped this description with Naturalisation's definition as a trap; only the Descent pairing in that question was correct.

qa.question: Real exam trap: What is Citizenship by Naturalisation, and what is Citizenship by Incorporation of Territory — and how did a real exam swap them?
qa.answer: Naturalisation: the Central Government grants a citizenship certificate to a foreigner (not an illegal migrant) who applies and meets residency/character qualifications (broadly ~12 years' residence in India under the Act's specific formula). Incorporation of Territory: if a new/foreign territory becomes part of India, the Government of India specifies by order which persons of that territory become Indian citizens. A real TNPSC question paired 'Naturalisation' with the Incorporation-of-Territory description (new territory becoming part of India) — incorrect pairing, a classic mode-swap trap.

qa.question: Real exam: What are the three ways an Indian can lose citizenship, and what specific grounds were tested together as all TRUE in one real question?
qa.answer: Three ways: Renunciation (voluntary giving up), Termination (automatically ceasing on voluntarily acquiring another country's citizenship, per Article 9/Section 9), and Deprivation (Government strips citizenship under specified grounds under Section 10 — e.g., fraud, disloyalty, trading with the enemy, imprisonment of 2+ years, or 7 continuous years' residence abroad without registering intent to retain citizenship). A real exam confirmed ALL THREE of: (1) voluntarily acquiring another country's citizenship, (2) being ordinarily resident abroad continuously for 7 years, and (3) being imprisoned for 2 years, as valid grounds.

qa.question: Does India recognise dual/multiple citizenship?
qa.answer: No — India recognises only SINGLE citizenship (one of the Constitution's 'unitary' features, unlike countries such as the USA which allow dual citizenship at the state and federal level). The Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) card is NOT full dual citizenship — it is a long-term visa-like status with limited rights, not equivalent to citizenship.

qa.question: What is the Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 (CAA), and what does it do?
qa.answer: Passed 11 December 2019, it provides an accelerated pathway to Indian citizenship for persecuted religious minorities — Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians — from Afghanistan, Bangladesh, and Pakistan who arrived in India by 2014. A significant, contested amendment to the 1955 Act's citizenship framework.

qa.question: What is an Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) card, and who is eligible?
qa.answer: A registration status (not full citizenship) for foreigners of Indian origin, or their spouses, granting benefits like the right to travel to, work in, and study in India. The CAA, 2019 also empowers the government to CANCEL an OCI registration if the holder violates any law notified by the central government.

qa.question: Procedurally, how does one apply for OCI registration?
qa.answer: The applicant submits their filled application (printed from the online form) to the District Collector/Magistrate of the area where they are ordinarily resident in India — or, if residing abroad, to the Indian Consular General's office.

questions.prompt: Citizenship is dealt with in which Articles of the Indian Constitution?
Which Article empowers Parliament to make laws regulating the acquisition and termination of citizenship?
The Citizenship Act, 1955 provides for how many modes of acquiring Indian citizenship?
questions.explanation: Five: Birth, Descent, Registration, Naturalisation, Incorporation of Territory.

questions.prompt: Which one of the following is correctly paired with its mode of acquiring citizenship? (1) Citizenship by Descent — a person born outside India on/after 26 Jan 1950 but before 10 Dec 1992, whose father was a citizen of India at the time of birth. (2) Citizenship by Registration — a foreigner, not an Indian migrant, can acquire citizenship simply on application for registration. (3) Citizenship by Naturalisation — if new territory becomes part of India, the Government specifies who among its people shall be citizens.
questions.explanation: Only (1) is correctly paired — (2) actually describes Naturalisation, and (3) actually describes Incorporation of Territory.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are valid ways of losing Indian citizenship under the Constitution? (1) Voluntarily acquiring the citizenship of another country. (2) Being ordinarily resident in any country for 7 years continuously. (3) Being imprisoned in any country for 2 years.
questions.explanation: All three are valid grounds (via termination and deprivation provisions).

questions.prompt: Under Article 9, what happens if an Indian citizen voluntarily acquires the citizenship of another country?
options: They must seek Presidential approval first
Only their voting rights are suspended

questions.prompt: The three broad ways of losing Indian citizenship are renunciation, termination, and:
Does the Indian Constitution recognise dual citizenship?
options: Yes, but only via the OCI card
questions.explanation: No — single citizenship only; OCI is not full dual citizenship.

questions.prompt: Citizenship by Naturalisation is granted to:
options: A foreigner (not an illegal migrant) who applies and meets residency/qualification conditions
Only minor children of Indian citizens
questions.explanation: A foreigner who applies and meets the Act's residency/qualification conditions.

questions.prompt: The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 (CAA) provides accelerated citizenship for persecuted religious minorities from which three countries?
Which of these religious communities is NOT named as eligible under the CAA, 2019?
questions.explanation: Muslims are not named; the eligible groups are Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians.

questions.prompt: The CAA, 2019 amendment additionally allows the government to:
options: Grant automatic citizenship to all foreigners
Cancel OCI registration if the holder violates a notified law
Abolish the OCI card scheme entirely
Grant dual citizenship to all NRIs
questions.explanation: Cancel OCI registration for violating a notified law.
